question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() method does not validate that maxTimestamp is less than the current consensus timestamp, so if the database contains future-dated record files (e.g., due to a clock skew attack on the consensus node), the retention window shifts forward and prunes token_account_history records that are still within the expected active window?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: hasNext()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that when findNextBetween() returns empty in the middle of iteration (line 137-139), the iterator silently sets current=null and returns false, causing the prune loop to exit without pruning the max record file's batch, leaving a gap in the pruning history that could cause the mirror node to serve inconsistent freeze state across the gap boundary?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune(RecordFileIterator)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the rate calculation 'long rate = elapsed > 0 ? countAfter / elapsed : 0L' in prune(RecordFileIterator) to cause a division by zero when elapsed is 0, which is caught by the outer try-catch in prune(), silently aborting the pruning loop mid-execution and leaving token_account_history in a partially pruned state where some freeze records are deleted but corresponding unfreeze records remain?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the shouldPrune() logic in RetentionProperties uses set membership checks on table names derived from class names, and by observing which tables are pruned via timing side-channels on the REST API, determine the exact retention configuration and predict when specific freeze/unfreeze records will be deleted, enabling a targeted attack window?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune(RecordFileIterator)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that repository.prune(endTimestamp) returns an int (row count) that is cast to long via 'long count = repository.prune(endTimestamp)', and if the actual deleted row count exceeds Integer.MAX_VALUE, the cast silently truncates the value, causing the counters map to undercount deletions and masking the fact that a catastrophically large number of token_account_history freeze records were deleted?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a minimum retention period enforcement in RetentionProperties to configure period=Duration.ofNanos(1), causing findLatestWithOffset(1) to return the record file just before the latest, and prune() to delete all token_account_history entries up to that point, permanently removing all freeze/unfreeze history and causing the mirror node to reconstruct all accounts as having default freeze status?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: hasNext()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the RecordFileIterator is initialized with 'new RecordFileIterator(latest.get())' where latest is fetched once before the loop, but during a long-running prune loop new record files are ingested, causing the max boundary to become stale and the iterator to prune token_account_history records that are within the new (shorter) safe retention window?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune(RecordFileIterator)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the executeWithoutResult() transaction wraps all repository prune calls, so if the database transaction isolation level is READ COMMITTED, a concurrent read of token_account_history during the prune transaction sees the pre-deletion state, but immediately after commit the freeze state is gone, creating a TOCTOU window where a downstream service caches the pre-deletion freeze status and serves it indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() method logs the retention period and maxTimestamp at INFO level, and by monitoring public log aggregation endpoints or error messages in API responses, determine the exact timestamp boundary being pruned, then time a token freeze transaction to land exactly at that boundary so it is immediately pruned on the next run?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune(RecordFileIterator)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune(RecordFileIterator) method does not check whether the endTimestamp being pruned is within the active freeze window of any token account, so it blindly deletes token_account_history rows whose timestamp_range overlaps with the current time, causing the mirror node to reconstruct an incorrect (frozen) state for accounts that were unfrozen after the pruned timestamp?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: hasNext()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findNextBetween uses an exclusive lower bound (consensus_end > ?1) and inclusive upper bound (consensus_end <= ?2), so if two record files have the same consensusEnd value (e.g., due to a data import bug), one is silently skipped, causing the iterator to miss the record file containing the unfreeze transaction and never pruning it, leaving the freeze record as the most recent state?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the @Scheduled annotation uses fixedDelayString with a SpEL expression '#{@retentionProperties.getFrequency().toMillis()}' evaluated at startup, so if retentionProperties is modified at runtime (it is a @Component with setters), the frequency change does not take effect until the next application restart, allowing an attacker who can influence configuration to set a very short frequency that causes rapid repeated pruning of token_account_history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune(RecordFileIterator)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the counters TreeMap in RecordFileIterator is shared across all iterations and uses Long::sum as the merge function, so if a repository throws an exception mid-iteration and the outer catch block logs the partial counters, the logged state appears to show successful pruning of token_account_history freeze records even though the transaction was rolled back, masking the true state?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() method does not acquire a distributed lock before running, so in a Kubernetes deployment with multiple importer pods, two pods can simultaneously call prune() with different maxTimestamp values, causing one pod to prune token_account_history up to timestamp T1 while another prunes up to T2 > T1, resulting in the second pod deleting unfreeze records that the first pod had not yet processed?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: getTableName()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getTableName() calls repository.getClass().getInterfaces()[0] without bounds checking, so if a repository implementation has no interfaces (e.g., a concrete class registered directly as a RetentionRepository via lambda or anonymous class), this throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, which is caught by the outer try-catch, aborting the prune loop and leaving token_account_history in a partially pruned state?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that retentionProperties.isEnabled() is checked at the start of prune() but not re-checked between batches, so if the enabled flag is toggled to false mid-run (e.g., via a configuration refresh), the current batch completes and token_account_history freeze records are deleted even though retention was disabled, causing permanent loss of freeze state?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune(RecordFileIterator)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune(RecordFileIterator) method uses 'long count = repository.prune(endTimestamp)' where the return type of RetentionRepository.prune() is int, and the implicit widening conversion does not detect negative return values from failed bulk deletes, causing the counters to accumulate incorrect values and masking catastrophic over-deletion of token_account_history freeze records?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: hasNext()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the RecordFileIterator.hasNext() method has a side effect (it modifies 'current'), violating the Iterator contract, so if hasNext() is called multiple times without an intervening next() call (e.g., by a for-each loop or stream), the iterator advances past record files containing unfreeze transactions, causing those transactions to be pruned at a later endTimestamp than intended?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() method uses 'var latest = recordFileRepository.findLatestWithOffset(retentionPeriod.toNanos())' where toNanos() on a large Duration can overflow long (Duration supports values larger than Long.MAX_VALUE nanoseconds), causing findLatestWithOffset to receive a negative offset and return the latest record file, making prune() delete all token_account_history including active freeze records?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: prune(RecordFileIterator)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the forEach loop over retentionRepositories inside executeWithoutResult() does not handle exceptions from individual repository.prune() calls, so if one repository throws a runtime exception, the entire transaction rolls back but the iterator's 'current' pointer has already advanced, causing the next prune() invocation to skip the failed batch and prune a later batch that includes active unfreeze records?
